subject: Internal medicine
title: Foreign body ingestion
preambleMarkdown:
# Foreign body ingestion



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Foreign body ingestion is a clinical condition or syndrome in internal medicine.
- This article focuses on adult presentation, differential diagnosis, diagnostics, complications, chronic management, and prevention.
- The practical clinical priority is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Foreign body ingestion to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Foreign body ingestion is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Foreign body ingestion depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In internal medicine, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Foreign body ingestion.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Foreign body ingestion disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Foreign body ingestion should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Foreign body ingestion first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Foreign body ingestion, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Foreign body ingestion, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused internal medicine examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Foreign body ingestion presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Foreign body ingestion with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Foreign body ingestion is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Foreign body ingestion should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Foreign body ingestion is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses internal medicine diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Foreign body ingestion, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Foreign body ingestion may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Foreign body ingestion patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Foreign body ingestion, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Foreign body ingestion in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Foreign body ingestion, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Foreign body ingestion does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Foreign body ingestion may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Foreign body ingestion, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Foreign body ingestion as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Foreign body ingestion draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Gastrointestinal Foreign Bodies

_Source lines: 9248-9365._

```text
III. GASTROINTESTINAL FOREIGN BODIES
The ingestion of foreign bodies is responsible for approximately 1,500 deaths each
year in the United States. Groups at risk include children, alcoholics, the mentally im-
paired, elderly with dementia, denture wearers, and prisoners.

A. Esophageal foreign bodies
1. Discussion. Eighty percent of patients with esophageal foreign bodies are chil-
dren. The child is typically brought to the ED by a caretaker after a witnessed
ingestion. If the ingestion was not witnessed, the diagnosis can be difficult.

2. Clinical features
a. Symptoms
i. Adults typically complain of a foreign body sensation in the throat or
chest. The patient may appear anxious and experience retching or vom-
iting, choking, coughing, or drooling.

ii. Children. Symptoms include refusal to eat, increased salivation, pain on
swallowing, vomiting, choking, and referred respiratory symptoms (e.g.,
stridor, cough, and wheezing).

b. Physical examination may reveal signs of infection. Fever and/or subcuta-
neous emphysema in the neck is suggestive of esophageal perforation.

## 3. Evaluation

a. Laryngoscopy. Direct or indirect laryngoscopy may be performed if the patient
feels that the foreign body is in the throat or if respiratory symptoms are present.

b. Radiography
i. Radiographs can be used to pinpoint the location of radiopaque objects
(e.g., coins, button batteries, sharp objects) (see Clinical Pearl 4-2).

ii. Pneumomediastinum or air in the soft tissues suggests perforation.

c. Endoscopy also allows localization of the object and is often therapeutic.

d. Esophagraphy. An esophagram should be performed only after consulting
with a specialist because the contrast material may interfere with later at-
tempts at endoscopy.

## 4. Therapy

a. Coins and small smooth objects often pass through the gastrointestinal tract
without difficulty. Coins that are too large to pass are removed by endos-
Quick 	HIT
Gastrografin, rather than
barium, should be used if a
perforation is suspected.

1. Coins in the esophagus align themselves in the frontal plane and are therefore seen face-on in an antero-
posterior view.

2. Coins in the trachea align themselves in the sagittal plane and are seen edge-on in an anteroposterior view.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	4-2
G A S T R O I N T E S T I N A L E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	115

Gastrointestinal Emergencies
copy. They can also be removed, by experienced physicians, using a Foley
catheter guided by fluoroscopy.

b. Button batteries have the potential to cause chemical corrosion and perfora-
tion of the esophagus and must be endoscopically removed as soon as possible.

c. Sharp objects must be removed by endoscopy.

d. Food impactions can be treated expectantly if the patient is managing his
or her secretions adequately. Medical interventions include the following:
i. Glucagon (1 mg intravenously) may relieve the impaction. A second 2-mg
dose can be administered in 20 minutes if the first dose is ineffective.

ii. Nitroglycerin (administered sublingually) may also assist in relieving
the impaction.

iii. Nifedipine (10 mg sublingually) relaxes the lower esophageal sphincter
and may allow passage of the food bolus.

iv. Diazepam may be used as a last resort.

v. Endoscopy should be performed if the bolus has not passed after sev-
eral hours.

5. Disposition. All patients need follow-up to rule out underlying esophageal
pathology.

B. Nonesophageal foreign bodies
1. Discussion. Once a foreign object has passed beyond the esophagus and into
the stomach, most will pass without complications.

2. Clinical features. Typically, nonesophageal foreign bodies are asymptomatic
unless perforation or impaction has occurred. Under these circumstances, the
patient presents with signs of peritonitis or obstruction.

3. Evaluation. Abdominal radiographs should be obtained to localize the object.

## 4. Therapy

a. Peritonitis or obstruction. Any patient with evidence of obstruction or per-
foration requires consultation with a surgeon.

b. Sharp objects that are in the stomach should be removed endoscopically
because they have a 15% to 35% chance of perforating the bowel if they are
not removed. If the sharp object has passed the pylorus, consultation with a
surgeon is required.

c. Other objects can be managed on an outpatient basis.

i. The stools should be checked for passage of the object, which usually
occurs in 48 to 72 hours but may take as long as 6 to 14 days. A re-
peat radiograph should be obtained in 1 week if the object has not yet
passed in the stool. If the object does not pass within 2 weeks, surgical
removal may be necessary.

ii. The patient should be advised to return immediately to the ED if he or
she experiences fever, abdominal pain, or vomiting.
```

### Source Excerpt: Foreign Bodies

_Source lines: 27361-27583._

```text
IV. FOREIGN BODIES
A. Discussion
1. Definition. A foreign body is any substance that is not natural to the body
passage in which it is found. Foreign bodies are common in infants and
young children because of their common exploration and experimentation
by putting things in the mouth, ears, or nasal passages, and occasionally in-
adequate supervision by adults. The majority of foreign bodies placed in the
mouth are swallowed. A smaller percentage of objects are aspirated into the
respiratory tree; approximately 50% of aspiration episodes are unwitnessed by
caregivers.

2. Severity. Although most foreign objects are expelled immediately by the cough
reflex and never require medical attention, foreign body injury is the most
common cause of injury-related death in children younger than 1 year of age.

Factors that influence the severity of the situation include:
a. Size of the foreign body. Large foreign objects that nearly or completely
occlude the upper airway pose an immediate life threat and must be emer-
gently removed.

b. Location of the foreign body. The location is directly related to the size of
the pediatric airway. The younger the child is, the greater the chance that
the site of obstruction will be more proximal. Large esophageal foreign bod-
ies can produce respiratory compromise due to compression of the adjacent
airway structures (see Clinical Pearl 15-3).

Quick 	HIT
Similar-aged infant siblings
have a 20-fold greater risk
of sudden infant death than
the general population and
should be offered interven-
tional monitoring or further
study for risk factors.

P E D I A T R I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	339

Pediatric Emergencies
c. Composition of the foreign object. Plant and other organic matter usu-
ally is much more tenacious and irritating than inorganic materials. Long,
sharp objects (such as sewing needles) pose a risk of perforation if in-
gested. Caustic materials, such as disc batteries, can cause erosion leading
to scarring and stenosis or perforation if they remain in the nose or gastro-
intestinal tract for a prolonged period of time.

B. Clinical findings. A high index of suspicion for foreign body aspiration must be
held for any child who presents with unexplained respiratory symptoms.

1. Many patients with aspiration events may have a symptom-free interval dur-
ing which the body attempts to wall off the object. Walling off leads to defini-
tive symptoms.

2. The symptoms, physical findings, and complication rate depend on the na-
ture of the aspirated foreign object, the location of the obstruction, and the
degree of obstruction.

a. Laryngeal foreign bodies cause an obstructive cough that becomes hoarse
and brassy, resembling the sound of croup.

i. This cough must be recognized quickly and managed correctly.

ii. Hot dogs and bread are the two most commonly aspirated substances
that lodge in the larynx. Bread and peanut butter are particularly irritat-
ing and tenacious, making removal difficult.

b. Tracheal foreign bodies cause cough and wheezing, intermittent cyanosis,
and a peculiar precordial thud, caused by the object impacting against the
subglottic area.

c. Bronchial foreign bodies often present with blood-streaked sputum,
cough , and dyspnea with or without wheezing or absent air sounds.

i. Initially, the child may be symptom-free, but later, unilateral wheezing
or prolongation of the expiratory phase may be noted.

ii. Edema and lodging of the foreign body in a smaller subsegmental air-
way may result in obstruction to outflow of air and air trapping or at-
electasis and mediastinal shift toward (with air trapping) or away from
(with atelectasis) the side of the obstructing foreign body.

C. Differential diagnoses
1. Pneumonia syndrome (see V.E). The sudden onset of symptoms will usu-
ally not confuse the astute clinician but may well be confused with more
protracted symptoms seen in smaller foreign body aspirations. Physical
examination findings are helpful only if air trapping or unilateral wheezing is
noted.

2. Croup syndrome (see V.A). The sudden onset of symptoms is often confused
with this entity acutely, but the lack of fever mitigates against the diagnosis.

3. Acute epiglottitis (see V.D) usually occurs in older children and is usually
heralded by high fever (i.e., greater than 103F), toxicity, and air hunger. Epi-
glottitis is rarely seen because of the widespread use of vaccine preparations
specific for Haemophilus influenzae.

D. Evaluation
1. History and physical examination. A suggestive history and confirmatory
physical examination are the most important diagnostic tools initially.

1. Esophageal foreign objects are most likely to become lodged at one of three sites of natural narrowing in
the esophagus: the thoracic inlet, the level of the aortic arch, and the gastroesophageal junction.

2. The larynx and cricopharyngeus muscle are the most common sites of airway involvement in children
younger than 1 year.

3. The trachea and bronchi are the most common sites involved in children between the ages of 15 and
48 months. Despite the acute angle of the left main stem bronchus, the propensity for the object lodging
in either side is approximately 50%.

PEARL	CLINICAL 	15-3
340 	 	S T E P - U P T O E M E R G E N C Y M E D I C I N E

Pediatric Emergencies
2. Imaging studies
a. Roentgenographic examination is truly useful only for radiopaque foreign
bodies but may be beneficial for asymmetric lung expansion or collapse.

b. Radiographic examination
i. General considerations
a) The plane in which the foreign object lies aids in differential location
of the object (Figure 15-1).

1) If the object is in the sagittal plane, it is probably in the larynx.

2) If in the coronal plane, it is probably in the esophagus.

b) The administration of contrast agents may also guide the location of
the foreign object if it is in the esophagus.

ii. Plain films are often helpful for diagnosing bronchial foreign bodies,
especially in patients with overinflation syndromes and collapse. If an
object causes complete obstruction in the expiratory phase but allows
air to pass in the inspiratory phase, air becomes trapped and hyperinfla-
tion is seen. This appears as a hyperlucency of the involved side.

c. Bronchoscopy is usually the only way to definitively diagnose and treat tra-
cheal foreign bodies
3. Laboratory studies are seldom helpful in the management of acute or subacute
obstructions with foreign bodies but may be useful with chronic obstructions
that were missed. Blood cultures are usually not warranted, except for chronic
foreign body ingestion of vegetable matter, particularly peanuts, which pro-
duces a cough, a septic type of fever, dyspnea, and chronic suppuration.

E. Therapy
1. Object removal. Patients with acute, life-threatening foreign body aspirations
should be treated emergently at the scene by either chest blows and thrusts or
abdominal thrusts, depending on the age of the patient, while attempting to
maintain oxygenation. Most ingested foreign bodies can be addressed none-
mergently; proximal esophageal foreign bodies causing respiratory compromise
require emergent endoscopy. Any foreign body remaining in the esophagus re-
quires urgent removal. Button batteries and long (! 5 cm), sharp objects (such
as sewing needles) pose special risks and require urgent surgical or gastroen-
terology consultation; if they are beyond the stomach, serial X-rays should be
obtained every 2 to 3 days to document progression and ultimate clearance of
the foreign body.

a. In a child who can cough, breathe, or speak, nonintervention is essen-
tial. A natural cough is more effective than airway or chest-compressive
maneuvers. Blind sweeps of the pharynx and hypopharynx in an awake and
Quick 	HIT
Bilateral decubitus chest
films can demonstrate
unilateral air trapping in a
patient who cannot cooper-
ate to perform inspiratory/
expiratory films.

F I G U R E
15-1 	CT chest showing foreign
body (whistle) in right
mainstem bronchus.

P E D I A T R I C E M E R G E N C I E S 	 	341

Pediatric Emergencies
alert child may induce vomiting and may further lodge the object deeper
into the airway, producing complete obstruction.

b. For a child younger than 1 year of age who is unresponsive, back blows
and chest thrusts are recommended in concert with attempts at ventilation.

c. For an unresponsive child older than 1 year, abdominal thrusts may be
performed in accordance with published standards in addition to attempts
at ventilation. Abdominal thrusts are not used for children younger than
1 year of age because of the risk of perforating the abdominal viscus.

d. Children in whom abdominal thrusts or chest thrusts fail to dislodge the
object may require advanced airway adjuncts to bypass the site of the ob-
struction until the object can be removed. Airway adjuncts include:
i. Needle cricothyrotomy
ii. Surgical cricothyrotomy
iii. Rigid bronchoscopy
iv. Open bronchotomy
e. Endoscopy. Children with more distal foreign bodies should undergo en-
doscopy as soon as practical. A delay doubles the morbidity and mortality.

f. Surgery may be necessary to remove the object.

2. Bronchodilator therapy is not recommended because of the risk of dislodging
a distal foreign body and having it lodge in a larger airway.

3. Antibiotic therapy may be indicated if a secondary infection has developed,
or in patients with subacute or chronic obstructions. Initiation of antibiotic
therapy should not delay the removal of foreign objects.

F. 	Disposition
1. Discharge. Children in whom the foreign body has been expelled, or in whom
a swallowed foreign body has entered the stomach, may be discharged to home
without further intervention or therapy except for accident counseling.

2. Admission. Children in whom surgical manipulation is necessary to clear ob-
structions warrant admission to the hospital for 24 hours or less.

3. Referral. Children with chronic unrecognized aspiration syndromes should
have consultation with not only an otolaryngologist but also a surgeon with
regard for the need for lobectomy.
```
